author: @darkmf666
text: Potter here... As for your theory about whether this material could be used to stabilize ceramic clays meant for firing. I kind of doubt that would work. Commercial clay bodies are specifically formulated for firing at certain temperatures. Sadly both magnesium and the sodium in the sodium silicate act as a ceramic flux at high temperatures. So probably when firing this at say 1200 degrees Celsius you will end up with a molten puddle of nastiness in the bottom of your kiln. But I do know that talcum is often added to low firing casting slips to aid quick drying with low shrinkage, it does ruin the plasticity at functional amounts, so it's really only useful for casting slips :(

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: If you mix it with aluminum sulfate instead of the magnesium oxide, it should chemically react more to displace the sodium ions and create more alumina-silicate material which does have a very high heat tolerance--ceramic fiber/fire blankets is essentially that material.  Hypothetically, mixing dry aluminum sulfate into a wet clay/sodium silicate mix, might also strengthen the structure some since when the A.S. reacts with the S.S., it will create needle and fibrous like structures within the clay?

text: Thank you so for your constant stimulating output. In my earlier days. When computer driven kilns came in we no longer had to be bone dry to load. We did essentially the equivalent of your thought by programming a 24 hour 200degree f preheat which produced the dry state , then the program would begin the bisque process up to about 1860f,  damage was minimal if any. Now the very curious thing about your process here is the hardness after the 180 degree oven. Shall I assume this was Fahrenheit? Finally do you have any thoughts on glaze or other surface treatments. What happens to the material in the video if you merely let it dry without baking? I look forward to your reply should you manage a moment, I know you are very busy indeed 🙏

author: @richardsandwell2285
text:
This is brilliant, I get a feeling that so much basic Chemistry is underused by people, some things are years old and have almost been forgotten about. 
At home I am having to be very brave I am working on trying to solve a unique problem, whilst trying to recycle a waste product into a long term and durable wall finish. 
Some time ago after fitting a wood-burning stove, I was left after the two new supporting lintels had been fitted with a bare brick chimney breast, the house has shockwave damage from a nearby blast from a WW2 bomb, so basically, any work involving walls invariably ends in a dusty mess because much of the plasterwork is cracked and just floating against the wall, the shockwave detached the bond to the brickwork. The bricks are not that great either, in fact, the houses were built in 1939 and were dubbed Jerry-built has an insult to the Germans.
Anyway, the bare brick chimney breast I covered in a strong render with a Chicken wire mesh to just tie the whole thing together and give it strength, after putting up with it and doing other jobs for a few years I decided what I wanted to do with it, I want irregular shapes and to be creative.
With the advent of Modified Polymer Adhesives, my go-to Favourite on this project has been Evostik "Sticks like Sh*t Turbo white" I have attached lots of Rocks to the wall and smaller pebbles, also I found translucent Quartz pebbles look amazing when backlit with LEDs and so does a product called GlasGlo made from old CRT screens. 
The whole thing looks totally amazing, Fossils have been cut in half from Lyme Regis bay and I have made them into up-lighter shelves, the LEDs are all drastically underrun so they should last me out, one or too rock shelves just emit black light and these have my Antique Uranium Glassware on them. 
Finally, I get to the point, I need to plaster around the rocks and over the many little low voltage wires that will eventually be powered by the Seebeck effect from TEGs from the stove heat itself.
PLASTER, at least the modern stuff does not allow much room for creativity, it sets too fast, it is hard to mould and work with the hands, you have to apply two layers it is limited to how thick you can apply it, lest it detaches and cracks.
Then I have access to mountains of waste paper especially newspapers at my work, apparently, much of it goes to landfill, so I thought about paper mache, but it must be fireproof, it must not get attacked by Fungus or insects, and acids in the paper can breakdown the cellulose chains in some papers. 
This is all a risk I am taking but it is a reasonably small area and not the entire room, however, I would like it to last me out and for it to almost mimic a natural chalk cliff with Rocks embedded in it.
My solution is as follows, I am making a paper slurry rich clay-like slurry I can hand apply to the walls. The ingredients are as follows Paper but I am going to try and use more white paper and old bank statements if I can, Sodium Bicarbonate, Solvite Wallpaper paste mainly for the inbuilt fungicide, PVA Glue, Kaolin clay, Zinc Oxide, Titanium Dioxide and of course water. 
The mix needs to be light enough to apply in quite a thick layer if needed around some rocks and thinner in other areas. Most plaster if applied thick will crack and fall off. 
Please tell me if you think I am bonkers, but they're just does not seem to be a product available via the building trade which will do this job, I wish I had your chemical knowledge, mine is a bit rusty these days.
Finally, if it lacks the desired whiteness and because PVA can cause emulsions to flake if I must paint on top of the Paper mache clay material, I will use an oil-based Alkali resistant primer, then an oil-based white undercoat then go to my choice of emulsion paint.
Maybe FWG could make a new type of plaster from paper mache that can be applied thick, is a good insulator, is fireproof and the best bit it should be able to be added to or removed without the dreadful dust the removing of old plaster generates of which I am all too well aware of in my 1939 bomb-damaged semi.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
As someone mentioned in one of the comments already, both sodium and magnesium ions act as flux's for ceramics--essentially they weaken the structure of the ceramic and make it "melt" at lower temperatures. 

  Instead of mixing in magnesium oxide, if you mix in aluminum sulfate instead, it should react a lot more with the sodium silicate, displacing more of the sodium ions and making alumina-silicate (essentially what ceramic fiber/fire blankets material is made of).  *

    I'm thinking of taking some basalt fiber cloth (reinforcing cloth for composites which similar strength properties as S2 grade fiberglass cloth, but higher heat tolerance) and putting a paste of clay+sodium silicate+aluminum sulfate in between two layers of the basalt cloth, to make a light'ish weight, mini rocket stove for backpacking use.  On the outside of the "composite clay" panels, I'll put a thin layer of high heat silicone to improve water resistance. Because the alumina silicate structures inside the clay, it should become more insulative when fully dried/cured, so that the silicone on the outside will most likely never get hot enough to degrade.  I have some special IR reflecting refractory coating (has a lot of zirconium oxide) that I'll probably line the inside of the stove with, to insure that this remains the case.

  *  This may also strengthen the structure some, since when these react, these produce needle fibrous like structures.  In any case, I might add a little diatomaceous earth and/or fumed silica as a high heat insulator and strengthener.  Also, when using aluminum sulfate, you might want to leave it wet for awhile and let it dry more at room temp before firing, just to make sure that more of it reacts and more of the sodium ions are displaced/replaced (? not sure which exactly happens).

I was trying to create a clay for home use on a wheel and use of a microwave instead of a kiln. I didn't go with the iron in microwave option for obvious reasons and used salak to varnish.... That was my fin project in product design studies 15 year ago 🤭.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: what about using something to shake around the smallest of the particles from regular sand to smash it up even further, or run a ball mill with basically some decent sized ball bearings (so you have magnetic material if any comes off that can be extracted with a magnet) to get even finer sand, then use regular clay for firing grey I assume to avoid what ever it is (iron oxide or some such) from contaminating your casting, and mixing it down to a runny substance called slip by all the ceramic shops I have ever talked to the people running them, and mix powdered regular dry clay into the sand, that way you can paint the surface of the item for your lost cast wax investment, with the slip and then place and shake, pack it down in the mixture of that ultra fine sand and powdered clay after mixing it with I guess moisture if needed or something, can not remember if it needed to be damp before packing down the "green sand" for the item inside it that you then fire in a kiln to drive out the wax and solidify the mold, though I do wonder if you could just use packed fine sand no ball milling and slip over the item that you then put into the sand and pack it down flat leaving your sprues sticking out. to pour in the liquid metal or what ever and let air out to avoid air bubbles being trapped?

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text:
whoops I have seen so many videos and instructional bits about lost wax casting using sand *same thing silicates come from so when I heard silicate I instantly just thought purified ultra small sand particulate that was bought, and thought this was about casting metal items out of say molten lead, (battery components?) or aluminum or something, LOL did not realize it was going to be a chemical form of clay forging to make ceramics ha ha, 
I should wonder though if you guys are still doing something with the lead for battery tech, and need more surface area in the lead that is easy though can be dangerous if you don't take care not to pour the molten lead too fast, as I found out by accident when I was younger about how to make lead brillo pad material LOL
ie I was trying to make pellets and had heard from someone who did not either know or realize that the aspect of dripping lead to get round bbs of it, requires a distance so it cools enough to go solid from liquid and thus they use water at the bottom to stop it with out it being deformed from hitting so hard, and all I was told was that they dripped it into water, and you got bbs so I tried this on the patio with molten lead and instead it danced around the top with bits of it sinking in making stringy formations of lead in the water, rather than the drop instantly going in as a bead, 
It just seems like if you controlled this maybe with a lid with holes in it, pour in molten lead that then drains through the holes it would make an immense amount of wirey lead that could then be mixed with what ever electrolyte in it and crushed into shape for a battery and get a LOT more surface area for your anode or cathode, or roll it flat with the stuff in and make sheets that have the electrolyte going down into the mass of thin wires of lead that all where contacting form a path as one of the poles again anode or cathode. 
just realize that since it is molten lead the reason it is dancing around on the surface of the water is because it is boiling the water and the molten bits are ejected back up by the steam so that if you go too fast it can explode and send molten lead everywhere along with steam and boiling water, and what ever lead salts form from the oxidizing with the oxygen in the water molecules or dissolved in the water, so it has to be very carefully controlled, I have experienced both so I know it can do this, and how damaging it can be.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: on the lead anodes and cathodes in the battery experiments I am wondering if you chose it because it is on the same row as carbon thus is supposed to be a neutral element able to accept 4 electrons or donate them away to get rid of the half full (to that magic number of 8) of electrons. I noticed this some time ago when looking for alternatives to carbon for making substances like graphene and nanotubes that were larger atoms for a different formation. lol.

author: @DanielSMatthews
text: G'Day Rob! Add some graphite and explore its properties, do you get a high-temperature electrode or even a heating element? As for clay "exploding" that is a complex topic as there are two forms of water in clay bodies, the free water and the chemically bound water, if the heating profile of the kiln is correct it will take the contents up through the different stages at a rate that avoids such catastrophic failures. Other contaminants will off-gas at different temperatures so you need to understand the individual chemistry of the clay you are using in order to program your electric kiln correctly. It makes you appreciate the genius of people who managed to get perfect results out of a wood-fired kiln centuries ago, doesn't it? As for ingredients, the crystal cat litter can be mixed with sodium hydroxide to make the water glass and the hydroxide can be made electrolytically from saltwater which also contains magnesium in the form of chlorides, so can you go directly from clay and sand at the beach plus seawater to a geopolymer, just by processing it in a well designed electrochemical cell? If you could do that, powered with solar power, and perhaps add plant fibres to make something similar to what is known as paper-clay then you would have an incredibly eco-friendly building and sculpting material, except for one problem which is hidden by just buying the sodium silicate, what do you do with the waste chlorine gas that the conversion of salt NaCl causes?

Mix it with sand for a nice casting medium.

replies.author: @carlospinheirotorres9499
replies.text: Please be cautious of misleading folks as @technosaurus3805 never suggested sodium hydroxide (NaOH) as casting medium, but the product of its reaction with silica (SiO2), which is the waterglass (Na2SiO3) they mention. In fact, this is perfectly safe to heat, as you may take from this video (Na2SiO3 is sodium silicate).

author: @reincarnatethylacine70
text: To prevent steam explosions pottery is usually fired more than once. Pieces at leather hardness are fired at a relatively low temperature which creates a usable but "soft"article. It is known as Bisque firing and can be quite porous. After cooling glaze is applied which is both decorative and impermeable. A second high temperature firing creates what we know as "Pottery". This is a brief and very generalised overview, the processes can be far more complex and is a fascinating skill to learn.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @NorthernKitty
text: "I now know far more about cat litter than I ever wanted to know..." says every cat owner everywhere!  😋  Thanks so much for this recipe!!  I was a little confused by the proportions used to make the water glass, though.  You said you mixed a 40% solution by weight,  but it was 120 grams of sodium silicate in a 500 milliliter solution with water?  I'm assuming that volume would actually result in less than 40% by weight, but that what really matters is the ratio of sodium silicate to clay and magnesium dioxide?  Thus, if I buy sodium silicate in a 40% by weight solution, I would need 300 grams of solution, but might want to add a little more water to make it easier to mix?  Please correct me if my math is wrong or I misunderstood.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - 40% is a fairly standard solution you can buy - I made a 40% solution up to 500mL because of the water absorption of the clay - it's the ratio of silicate to clay that matters - the amount of water doesn't really - so tbh I think you have got it - plus the range is something like 10-40% - so it's not that critical anyway

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks!!  Sorry, I get bogged down on precision and absolutes sometimes to my detriment - I should trust my gut more to understand something.  This is why I don't cook, LOL.

author: @earlyriser438
text:
Excellent

Quite nice sculpting skills Robert, you're too humble.

Might I suggest a thoroughly excellent book if you're interested in flexing those artistic muscles some more? Lots of neat chemistry at play to get some real stunning finishes, the book gives all the recipes times and temperatures.

"Alternative Kilns and Firing Techniques: Raku * Saggar * Pit * Barrel" by James C. Watkins

https://www.amazon.ca/gp/aw/d/1579909523?psc=1&ref=ppx_pop_mob_b_asin_title

author: @The-Green-Manali-shi
text:
I do a bit of sculpting and mix my own air dry clay using, 1 ROLL Toilet paper,4 SPOONS Vinegar, 1 CUP White Glue, 1 CUP of CHEAP TILE ADHESIVE,  1 CUP BI CARB SODA, 3 TABLE SPOONS BABY OIL, MIX ALL ABOVE, 1 CUP FLOUR, MIX ALL ABOVE, ADD MORE FLOUR UNTIL RIGHT, KNEED BY HAND
I don't have a kiln YET, I will give this a go as it is a good alternative for standard clay (WHEN I GET TIME).
Thanks again Rob. ✔✔✔✔✔✔

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like that recipe mate - do you mind if I steal it and make a video? - it looks too useful to sit here in the comments section - due credit - of course

replies.author: @The-Green-Manali-shi
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes, please do. I bet you can improve on it, feel free to try. The toilet paper needs ripping up and soaking in water before use. I put it in a bowl of water and whisk it until it is in small pieces. The clay dries very hard when used, it keeps in a sealed plastic bag for a long time. The creations are not good for outdoor use unless sealed with an exterior varnish.

replies.author: @The-Green-Manali-shi
replies.text: Sorry, just to add... Ring out the toilet paper after soaking to form a moist ball then break up.

replies.author: @Scipiworld
replies.text: Not sure about the final product, but magnesium oxide is one of the most thermally resistant materials (without getting into super exotic materials). It can handle temperatures as high as 2500C. I would imagine a high MgO mixture with the right polymerization behavior could make a very resistant material if supported with a frame.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not really sure mate but I would expect up to 600 degrees C - still worth a look around on google scholar to confirm this

replies.author: @nedisawegoyogya
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I saw many people use sodium silicate as firebrick binder on youtube

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@nedisawegoyogya   Sodium and potassium act as flux for ceramics and glass unfortunately, which means they weaken these materials and lower the melting temps.  (This is why wood fires eventually degrade things like fire bricks etc, because of the potassium etc in the wood). 

     But if the sodium silicate can react with something and lose the sodium ions (to become something like alumino or magnesium siliciates), then it will be fine.  I'm not sure how much chemical reaction is going on with this combo.

replies.author: @nedisawegoyogya
replies.text: @justinw1765 sodium or potassium act as charge balancer allowing polymerization. If you heat up geopolymer it becomes crystalline material covalently bonded. While it will act as "flux", it is only effective in the melting point region of igneous rock because of the chemical composition. Indeed without the sodium, clay bricks are mostly composed of mullite and have a melting point of 1840°C whereas granite 1240°C. But if we are talking about pizza ovens both will do.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Very good. You could review information on pottery glazing and on hobbyist making of slip-cast ceramics.  It is possible to get very precisely shaped products, and to use some forms in metal casting and mold forming.  There's some brands of cat litter that are almost 100% sodium silicate, and you can use potassium or sodium hydroxide to reclaim it cheaply in large quantities.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Great suggestion!

replies.author: @azlandpilotcar4450
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I leach wood ash from my winter fireplace for lye. Leftover charcoal and ash make great soil and aggregate amendment.  My art-degreed (Bachelor arts - ceramics) daughter tells me that lye and wood ash are some of the oldest pottery glazes.  She and my wife used to do hobby "porcelain" from slip-cast ceramics.  The pottery must be dried thoroughly, although often at very low heat, before glazing and firing.  Glaze must also dry, and must be fired at very high temperatures.  I think potassium and sodium salt glazes are interesting -- hard, cheap and quite waterproof.  As you say, this is very, very old technology - arguably the first industrial manufacturing process, even predating smelting and glass.  Your "Sorrel" (spelling?) cements seem to me to be very much like porcelain slip.  Sugru might make a great slip molding material.

Speaking of Cat litters:
Another cat litter is nearly pure Sodium Silicate Gell in small bead form. Just like the moisture desiccant packets.
This is a a bit more expansive than normal litters.
I used it to make refractory cement.
